abstract:
For nearly a century, the Fischer-Tropsch (FT) reaction has remained at the center of intense scientific debate. Despite its industrial maturity, the precise molecular nature of the active sites and the underlying reaction mechanisms for both cobalt- and iron-based catalysts continue to be refined. Over the past 15 years, the rise of "bottom-up" approaches—integrating surface science experiments with high-level molecular modeling—has brought a clearer molecular picture into focus. 

Theoretical models have established a detailed structural framework for cobalt catalyst particles, while recent density functional theory (DFT) calculations and surface science studies have underscored the critical role of realistic surface coverages. These coverages are now known to induce surface reconstructions that fundamentally alter the stability of reaction intermediates. For cobalt-based FT synthesis, a combination of mechanistic experiments and detailed microkinetic simulations is driving the field toward a consensus regarding the active sites and the catalytic cycle. 

In contrast, iron-based catalysts present a greater challenge due to their dynamic phase evolution under reaction conditions, which obscures the identification of stable surface structures and active sites. Modern computational techniques are beginning to manage the combinatorial complexity of these evolving iron systems. While recent experimental and DFT research has probed the mechanistic pathways of iron-based catalysts, the current lack of a definitive structural model for the active site continues to limit the development of a comprehensive molecular theory. Finally, the direct hydrogenation of CO₂ into long-chain hydrocarbons is emerging as a critical research frontier, offering a sustainable future for FT synthesis within a circular carbon economy.
